title: Ensure the default security group of every VPC restricts all traffic

input_row.title: Ensure the default security group of every VPC restricts all traffic
input_row.description: A VPC comes with a default security group whose initial settings deny all inbound traffic, allow all outbound traffic, and allow all traffic between instances assigned to the security group. If a security group is not specified when an instance is launched, it is automatically assigned to this default security group. Security groups provide stateful filtering of ingress/egress network traffic to AWS resources. It is recommended that the default security group restrict all traffic, both inbound and outbound. The default VPC in every region should have its default security group updated to comply with the following: • No inbound rules. • No outbound rules. Any newly created VPCs will automatically contain a default security group that will need remediation to comply with this recommendation. Note: When implementing this recommendation, VPC flow logging is invaluable in determining the least privilege port access required by systems to work properly, as it can log all packet acceptances and rejections occurring under the current security groups. This dramatically reduces the primary barrier to least privilege engineering by discovering the minimum ports required by systems in the environment. Even if the VPC flow logging recommendation in this benchmark is not adopted as a permanent security measure, it should be used during any period of discovery and engineering for least privileged security groups.
input_row.rationale: Configuring all VPC default security groups to restrict all traffic will encourage the development of least privilege security groups and promote the mindful placement of AWS resources into security groups, which will, in turn, reduce the exposure of those resources.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if the account is configured as prescribed: Security Group State 1. Login to the AWS VPC Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/home. 2. Repeat the following steps for all VPCs, including the default VPC in each AWS region: 3. In the left pane, click Security Groups. 4. For each default security group, perform the following: o Select the default security group. o Click the Inbound Rules tab and ensure no rules exist. o Click the Outbound Rules tab and ensure no rules exist. Security Group Members 1. Login to the AWS VPC Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/home. 2. Repeat the following steps for all default groups in all VPCs, including the default VPC in each AWS region: 3. In the left pane, click Security Groups. 4. Copy the ID of the default security group. 5. Change to the EC2 Management Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/v2/home. 6. In the filter column type Security Group ID : <security-group-id-from- step-4>.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to implement the prescribed state: Security Group Members 1. Identify AWS resources that exist within the default security group. 2. Create a set of least-privilege security groups for those resources. 3. Place the resources in those security groups, removing the resources noted in step 1 from the default security group. From Console: 1. Login to the AWS VPC Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/home. 2. Repeat the following steps for all VPCs, including the default VPC in each AWS region: 3. In the left pane, click Security Groups. 4. For each default security group, perform the following: o Select the default security group. o Click the Inbound Rules tab. o Remove any inbound rules. o Click the Outbound Rules tab. o Remove any Outbound rules. From Command Line: 1. List all default security groups in the specified region aws ec2 describe-security-groups --region <region-name> --query 'SecurityGroups[?GroupName == `default`]' 2. Check if the inbound rules (IpPermissions) and outbound rules (IpPermissionsEgress) of the default security group are empty. If the rules are not empty, proceed and note down the Security Group ID (GroupId) of the security group with non-empty rules. 3. List the inbound security group rule IDs (SecurityGroupRuleId) aws ec2 describe-security-group-rules --region <region-name> --query 'SecurityGroupRules[?GroupId == `<default-security-group-id>` && IsEgress == `false`]' 4. Delete the inbound security group rules based on their rule IDs aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <default-security-group-id> --security-group-rule-ids <inbound-rule-id-1> <inbound-rule-id-2> 5. List the outbound security group rule IDs (SecurityGroupRuleId) aws ec2 describe-security-group-rules --region <region-name> --query 'SecurityGroupRules[?GroupId == `<default-security-group-id>` && IsEgress == `true`]' 6. Delete the outbound security group rules based on their rule IDs aws ec2 revoke-security-group-egress --group-id <default-security-group-id> --security-group-rule-ids <outbound-rule-id-1> <outbound-rule-id-2> Recommended IAM groups allow you to edit the "name" field. After remediating default group rules for all VPCs in all regions, edit this field to add text similar to "DO NOT USE. DO NOT ADD RULES."
input_row.impact: Implementing this recommendation in an existing VPC that contains operating resources requires extremely careful migration planning, as the default security groups are likely enabling many ports that are unknown. Enabling VPC flow logging (for accepted connections) in an existing environment that is known to be breach-free will reveal the current pattern of ports being used for each instance to communicate successfully. The migration process should include: • Analyzing VPC flow logs to understand current traffic patterns. • Creating least privilege security groups based on the analyzed data. • Testing the new security group rules in a staging environment before applying them to production.
input_row.default_value: By default, each VPC has a “default” security group that allows all outbound traffic and all traffic between resources within the group. No restrictions are enforced unless manually configured.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit can be carried out using AWS APIs that allow listing security groups, checking their inbound and outbound rules, and deleting rules if necessary. 
- The audit process includes clear commands available through the AWS CLI and methods via the boto3 SDK, confirming that these properties can indeed be accessed programmatically.
- The audit and remediation steps provided in the JSON offer explicit commands to follow, which further reinforces the ability to automate this audit.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_ec2_security_group_default_rules_restriction_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the following AWS CLI command to list all default security groups in each region:
  ```
  aws ec2 describe-security-groups --region <region-name> --query 'SecurityGroups[?GroupName == `default`]'
  ```
- Next, check the inbound rules using the command:
  ```
  aws ec2 describe-security-group-rules --region <region-name> --query 'SecurityGroupRules[?GroupId == `<default-security-group-id>` && IsEgress == `false`]'
  ```
  - Expected result: No inbound rules should be present; an empty list indicates compliance.
  
- For outbound rules, check similarly:
  ```
  aws ec2 describe-security-group-rules --region <region-name> --query 'SecurityGroupRules[?GroupId == `<default-security-group-id>` && IsEgress == `true`]'
  ```
  - Expected result: No outbound rules should be present; again, an empty list indicates compliance.

- If any rules exist, delete them using the following commands:
  - For inbound rules:
    ```
    aws ec2 revoke-security-group-ingress --group-id <default-security-group-id> --security-group-rule-ids <inbound-rule-id-1> <inbound-rule-id-2>
    ```
  
  - For outbound rules:
    ```
    aws ec2 revoke-security-group-egress --group-id <default-security-group-id> --security-group-rule-ids <outbound-rule-id-1> <outbound-rule-id-2>
    ```

- The expected Pass criteria for this audit is that both inbound and outbound rules lists for the default security group should be empty. If the lists contain any entries, it would indicate non-compliance.

By performing these checks automatically across all relevant AWS regions and VPCs, it enables efficient and consistent compliance monitoring and enforcement regarding the default security groups' configurations.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit as Automated is correct. AWS provides APIs and CLI commands that can be used to programmatically list and modify security group rules, making this task suitable for automation.
- The use of AWS CLI and boto3 SDK is appropriate for this audit, as they both support operations on security groups, including listing and modifying rules.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are mostly correct. The AWS CLI commands provided are valid for listing security groups and their rules. However, the `describe-security-group-rules` command is not directly available in AWS CLI; instead, the `describe-security-groups` command should be used with filters to check rules.
- The program name `aws_ec2_security_group_default_rules_restriction_check` is descriptive, but could be more precise by including the term 'default' to emphasize the focus on default security groups.
- The commands for revoking ingress and egress rules are correct, but the method to retrieve rule IDs for deletion is not explicitly mentioned.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The command `aws ec2 describe-security-group-rules` should be replaced with `aws ec2 describe-security-groups` with appropriate filtering to check rules.
- Provide a method to retrieve the rule IDs for deletion, such as using the `--query` parameter to extract rule IDs from the `describe-security-groups` output.
- Consider using AWS Config to continuously monitor security group configurations for compliance.
- A better program name could be `aws_ec2_default_security_group_restriction_check` to clearly indicate the focus on default security groups.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-structured and mostly accurate, with minor improvements needed in command usage and program naming.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the command usage to correctly reflect the available AWS CLI capabilities, specifically replacing `describe-security-group-rules` with `describe-security-groups` and include details on extracting rule IDs for deletion.
- Consider renaming the program for clarity and precision.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using AWS APIs and CLI commands. The AWS CLI and boto3 SDK provide the necessary capabilities to list and modify security group rules programmatically. The task involves checking the default security group's inbound and outbound rules, which can be efficiently handled using these tools.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_ec2_default_security_group_restriction_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: EC2 Security Groups
- API/CLI Method: 
  - `aws ec2 describe-security-groups` to list security groups and filter for default groups.
  - Use filtering to check for inbound and outbound rules.
- Properties to Check: 
  - `GroupName` for identifying default security groups.
  - `IpPermissions` and `IpPermissionsEgress` for checking inbound and outbound rules.
- Pass Criteria: No inbound (`IpPermissions`) and outbound (`IpPermissionsEgress`) rules should exist for default security groups.
- Fail Criteria: Presence of any inbound or outbound rules in the default security group indicates non-compliance.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** Both AI opinions were in agreement regarding the automation capability. The second opinion provided a correction on the CLI command usage, which has been incorporated into the final decision.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Amazon_Web_Services_Foundations_Benchmark_v6.0.0
